attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman at 34 weeks gestation presents to the Emergency Department with mild right upper quadrant abdominal pain. Her vital signs are stable with blood pressure 110/70 mmHg. Blood tests are performed as part of her workup:

Alkaline phosphatase: 350 U/L (30-130)
Alanine aminotransferase: 22 U/L (5-40)
Aspartate aminotransferase: 18 U/L (5-40)
Total bilirubin: 8 μmol/L (3-20)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best explains the alkaline phosphatase result in this patient?
attributes.explanation:
The elevated alkaline phosphatase level of 350 U/L in this third-trimester pregnant woman is due to placental production. During normal pregnancy, alkaline phosphatase levels increase two to fourfold, primarily from placental synthesis. The upper limit of normal increases from approximately 130 U/L in the first trimester to over 400 U/L in the third trimester. This patient's level of 350 U/L is within the expected range for a woman at 34 weeks gestation.

The other liver function tests are normal, supporting that this is a physiological change rather than pathological.

The incorrect options can be explained:

  • Cholestasis of pregnancy would typically present with pruritus and more significantly elevated liver enzymes, particularly bile acids
  • Acute fatty liver of pregnancy is a serious condition that would present with symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and more significant liver function abnormalities
  • Pre-eclampsia typically presents with hypertension (blood pressure ≥140/90 mmHg) and proteinuria. This patient has normal blood pressure (110/70 mmHg) and while liver enzymes may be elevated in pre-eclampsia with severe features, isolated alkaline phosphatase elevation is not characteristic
  • Bone turnover does increase during pregnancy but contributes much less to the alkaline phosphatase elevation than placental production

It's important to note that bilirubin concentration does not significantly change during normal pregnancy, which is consistent with this patient's normal bilirubin level.
learningPoints: Alkaline phosphatase levels increase two to fourfold during normal pregnancy, primarily due to placental production.
The upper limit of normal for alkaline phosphatase increases from approximately 130 U/L in the first trimester to over 400 U/L in the third trimester.
Bilirubin concentration does not significantly change during normal pregnancy.
Pre-eclampsia typically presents with hypertension, proteinuria, and may include elevated liver enzymes, but alkaline phosphatase elevation alone is not diagnostic.
references: Nelson-Piercy C. Handbook of obstetric medicine. 6th ed. Boca Raton: CRC Press; 2021
